cards.definition: a set of step-by-step instructions to solve a problem — must be precise, unambiguous, and finite
a way of writing algorithms using plain English and simple code-like structures — not a real programming language
a diagram showing an algorithm using standard shapes — ovals (start/end), rectangles (process), diamonds (decision)
instructions carried out one after another in order — the most basic control structure
a decision point in an algorithm — IF...THEN...ELSE — the program chooses which path to take
repetition — a section of code that loops — FOR loops, WHILE loops, DO-UNTIL loops
searching through items one by one from the start — simple but inefficient for large datasets
dividing the sorted dataset in half repeatedly until the item is found — much faster than linear search
a sorting algorithm — repeatedly swaps adjacent items if they are in the wrong order — simple but inefficient
a sorting algorithm — divides the list in half, sorts each half, then merges — more efficient than bubble sort

questions.question: What is an algorithm?
options: B) A set of step-by-step instructions to solve a problem
C) A type of data structure
questions.explanation: An algorithm is a precise set of steps that solves a problem. It must be finite, unambiguous, and effective.

questions.question: What is the difference between selection and iteration?
options: B) Selection is for loops; iteration is for decisions
C) Selection is a decision (IF); iteration is repetition (loops)
D) Selection uses FOR loops; iteration uses IF
questions.explanation: Selection: IF...THEN...ELSE — the program chooses a path. Iteration: loops (FOR, WHILE, DO-UNTIL) — code repeats.

questions.question: What does a diamond shape represent in a flowchart?
questions.explanation: Diamond = decision/condition. Rectangle = process. Oval = start/end. Parallelogram = input/output.

questions.question: Which search algorithm requires the data to be sorted first?
questions.explanation: Binary search requires a sorted list — it divides the list in half each time, which only works if items are in order.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of binary search?
questions.explanation: Binary search is O(log n) — each step halves the search space. Linear search is O(n) — must check each item.

questions.question: What is bubble sort?
options: A) Repeatedly divides the list and merges sorted halves
B) Repeatedly swaps adjacent items if they are in wrong order
C) Finds the minimum item and moves it to the front
D) Searches for an item using a middle value
questions.explanation: Bubble sort repeatedly compares and swaps adjacent elements — large values 'bubble' to the end. It is simple but O(n²) — inefficient for large lists.

questions.question: Why is merge sort more efficient than bubble sort?
options: B) It is easier to code
C) Its time complexity is O(n log n) vs O(n²) for bubble sort
questions.explanation: Merge sort is O(n log n) — divide and conquer. Bubble sort is O(n²) — very slow for large datasets.

questions.question: What is pseudocode?
options: C) Code written in a specific language
D) An informal way of writing algorithms using plain English and code-like structures
questions.explanation: Pseudocode is a way to plan algorithms without worrying about syntax — it uses plain English combined with code structures like IF, FOR, WHILE.

questions.question: What is a linear search?
options: A) Searching by dividing in half
B) Searching through items one by one from the start
questions.explanation: Linear search checks each item in sequence. It is simple and works on unsorted data, but is slow for large datasets — O(n).

questions.question: What are the three basic programming constructs?
questions.explanation: All programs are built from three constructs: sequence (in order), selection (decisions), and iteration (repetition).

questions.question: An algorithm must be finite — it must have an end.
questions.explanation: An infinite algorithm would never solve the problem. Algorithms must terminate.

questions.question: Binary search works on unsorted data.
questions.explanation: Binary search REQUIRES sorted data — it divides the list in half, which only works if items are ordered.

questions.question: Selection in programming is represented by IF...THEN...ELSE.
questions.explanation: Selection allows programs to make decisions and follow different paths based on conditions.

questions.question: Bubble sort is more efficient than merge sort for large datasets.
questions.explanation: Bubble sort is O(n²) — very slow for large lists. Merge sort is O(n log n) — much more efficient.

questions.question: A flowchart diamond represents a decision.
questions.explanation: Diamond = yes/no decision. Rectangle = process. Oval = start/end.

questions.question: Iteration means repeating a section of code.
questions.explanation: Iteration includes FOR loops, WHILE loops, and DO-UNTIL loops.

questions.question: Linear search can only work on sorted data.
questions.explanation: Linear search works on unsorted data — it checks each item one by one.

questions.question: Pseudocode is a real programming language.
questions.explanation: Pseudocode is an informal planning tool — it uses English and code-like structures but is not executable.

questions.question: Merge sort uses a divide and conquer approach.
questions.explanation: Merge sort divides the list in half, recursively sorts each half, then merges — O(n log n).

questions.question: Sequence means instructions are carried out one after another.
questions.explanation: Sequence is the most basic construct — instructions execute in order, one at a time.

questions.question: An ________ is a precise set of step-by-step instructions to solve a problem.
questions.explanation: Algorithms must be finite, unambiguous, and effective.

questions.question: ________ is an informal way of writing algorithms using plain English.
questions.explanation: Pseudocode helps plan code without worrying about specific language syntax.

questions.question: ________ is a decision point — IF...THEN...ELSE.
questions.explanation: Selection allows the program to choose different paths based on conditions.

questions.question: ________ is repetition — FOR, WHILE, or DO-UNTIL loops.
questions.explanation: Iteration repeats a block of code until a condition is met.

questions.question: ________ search checks each item one by one — simple but slow for large datasets.
questions.explanation: Linear search is O(n) — it may have to check every item.

questions.question: ________ search divides a sorted list in half repeatedly — O(log n).
questions.explanation: Binary search is much faster than linear search but requires sorted data.

questions.question: ________ sort repeatedly swaps adjacent items in the wrong order — O(n²).
questions.explanation: Bubble sort is simple but inefficient for large datasets.

questions.question: ________ sort divides the list, sorts each half, and merges — O(n log n).
questions.explanation: Merge sort is more efficient than bubble sort for large datasets.

questions.question: A ________ shape in a flowchart represents a decision.
questions.explanation: Diamond = yes/no. Rectangle = process. Oval = start/end.

questions.question: The three basic constructs are sequence, ________, and iteration.
questions.explanation: All programs are built from these three constructs.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: step-by-step instructions to solve a problem
checks each item one by one — O(n)
divides sorted list in half — O(log n)
swaps adjacent items — simple but O(n²)
divide and conquer — more efficient O(n log n)

cards.definition: a base-2 number system using only 0s and 1s — the fundamental language of computers
the smallest unit of data — a single 0 or 1
8 bits — the standard unit of data storage
American Standard Code for Information Interchange — assigns a number to each character
the smallest element of a digital image — each pixel is stored as a binary colour value
in audio — the number of samples taken per second — higher = better quality but larger file
the number of bits transmitted per second — affects sound/video quality and file size
reducing file size — lossless (no quality lost) or lossy (quality reduced)
two or more computers connected to share data and resources
a unique numerical address identifying a device on a network — allows routing of data

questions.question: What is binary?
options: B) A base-2 number system using 0s and 1s
C) A type of data compression
D) A type of network protocol
questions.explanation: Binary (base-2) uses only 0 and 1 — all data in computers is stored and processed in binary.

questions.question: How many bits are in a byte?
questions.explanation: 1 byte = 8 bits. 1 kilobyte = 1024 bytes. 1 megabyte = 1024 KB.

questions.question: What is a pixel?
options: A) A unit of sound quality
B) The smallest element of a digital image
C) A type of binary code
D) A measure of file size
questions.explanation: A pixel is the smallest unit of a digital image. Each pixel is stored as binary values for red, green, and blue.

questions.question: What is the difference between lossless and lossy compression?
options: A) Lossless reduces quality; lossy doesn't
B) Lossless preserves all data; lossy permanently removes some data
C) Lossless is for images only
questions.explanation: Lossless: all original data preserved (e.g. PNG, ZIP). Lossy: some data permanently removed for smaller file size (e.g. JPEG, MP3).

questions.question: What does a higher sampling rate mean for audio?
options: C) Better quality but larger file size
questions.explanation: Higher sampling rate = more samples per second = more accurate representation = better quality but larger file size.

questions.question: What is an IP address?
options: B) A unique numerical address identifying a device on a network
D) A type of data compression
questions.explanation: Every device on a network has a unique IP address — it is used for routing data packets to the correct destination.

questions.question: What is ASCII?
options: B) A standard assigning numbers to characters
questions.explanation: ASCII assigns a number (0–127) to each character (letters, digits, symbols). 'A' = 65, 'a' = 97.

questions.question: What is bit rate?
options: A) Number of bits per byte
C) Number of bits transmitted per second
questions.explanation: Bit rate measures data transmission speed — higher bit rate = better quality audio/video but larger file size.

questions.question: What is a network?
options: B) Two or more computers connected to share data and resources
D) A type of data compression
questions.explanation: A network connects two or more devices — allowing sharing of files, hardware (printers), and internet access.

questions.question: What is compression used for?
options: B) Transmitting data faster by reducing file size
questions.explanation: Compression reduces file size — making storage more efficient and transmission faster.

questions.question: Binary is a base-10 number system.
questions.explanation: Binary is a base-2 number system using only 0 and 1.

questions.question: One byte equals 8 bits.
questions.explanation: 8 bits = 1 byte. 1024 bytes = 1 kilobyte.

questions.question: Lossy compression permanently removes some data.
questions.explanation: Lossy compression (JPEG, MP3) reduces file size by permanently discarding some data — quality is reduced.

questions.question: A higher sampling rate produces smaller audio files.
questions.explanation: Higher sampling rate = more samples = LARGER file size (but better quality).

questions.question: An IP address uniquely identifies a device on a network.
questions.explanation: Every networked device has a unique IP address used for routing data packets.

questions.question: ASCII assigns numbers to characters.
questions.explanation: ASCII: 'A' = 65, 'a' = 97, '0' = 48. It allows text to be represented as binary numbers.

questions.question: Lossless compression reduces quality.
questions.explanation: Lossless compression preserves ALL original data — file is smaller but quality is unchanged.

questions.question: A pixel is the smallest unit of a digital image.
questions.explanation: Each pixel stores colour values in binary — more pixels = higher resolution.

questions.question: Bit rate measures the number of samples taken per second.
questions.explanation: Bit rate = bits per second (transmission speed). Sampling rate = samples per second (audio quality).

questions.question: A network requires at least two connected devices.
questions.explanation: A network connects two or more computers to share data and resources.

questions.question: ________ is a base-2 number system using only 0s and 1s.
questions.explanation: All data in computers is ultimately stored and processed as binary.

questions.question: 1 ________ = 8 bits.
questions.explanation: 1 kilobyte = 1024 bytes. 1 megabyte = 1024 kilobytes.

questions.question: A ________ is the smallest element of a digital image.
questions.explanation: Pixels store colour values in binary — three values for red, green, and blue.

questions.question: ________ assigns a unique number to each character.
questions.explanation: ASCII allows text to be stored as binary numbers in a computer.

questions.question: ________ compression preserves all original data — no quality loss.
questions.explanation: Examples: PNG (images), ZIP (files). Lossless is larger than lossy.

questions.question: ________ compression permanently removes some data to reduce file size.
questions.explanation: Examples: JPEG (images), MP3 (audio). Quality is reduced.

questions.question: ________ rate measures the number of audio samples per second.
questions.explanation: Higher sampling rate = better audio quality but larger file.

questions.question: ________ rate measures bits transmitted per second.
questions.explanation: Higher bit rate = better quality audio/video but larger file size.

questions.question: An ________ address is a unique numerical identifier for a network device.
questions.explanation: IP addresses allow routers to direct data packets to the correct destination.

questions.question: A ________ connects two or more computers to share data and resources.
questions.explanation: Networks allow sharing of files, internet access, and hardware like printers.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: reducing file size — lossless or lossy
unique identifier for a device on a network
audio samples per second — higher = better quality, larger file
